coredata.dc:title: Effect of surface charge on peroxidase mimetic activity of gold nanorods (GNRs)
coredata.dc:creator: Karthiga, D.
Choudhury, Samraggi
Chandrasekaran, N.
Mukherjee, Amitava
coredata.dc:description: In the present work, we have studied the impact of surface charge on the peroxidase mimetic activity of gold nanorods (GNRs). Gold nanorods with positive (cetyltrimethylammonium bromide (CTAB)) and negative (poly (styrene sulfonic acid) (PSS)) surface charges were synthesized and subsequently characterized by UV–visible spectroscopy, zeta potential analysis, and transmission electron microscopy (TEM). The two kinds of gold nanorods were evaluated for their peroxidase mimetic activity. Gold nanorods with different surface charges can able to mimic the enzyme activity near acidic pH over a wide range of temperatures. Poly (4-styrene sulfonic acid)-stabilized negative gold nanorods (N-GNRs) possessed higher peroxidase mimetic activity compared to CTAB-stabilized positive GNRs (P-GNRs) when tested with a low concentration of tetramethylbenzidine (1 mM) and hydrogen peroxide (0.1 M). The significant impact of surface charges of GNRs on the catalytic reactions may help us to make a prototype in creating nanozymes as peroxidase mimics.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Gold nanorods
Hydrogen peroxide
Peroxidase mimetics
Surface charge
Tetramethylbenzidine

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Procedure for peroxidase mimetic activity measurement
Synthesis and characterization of P and N-GNRs
Effect of H2O2 and substrate concentration on the product with time
Peroxidase mimetic activity of P and N-GNRs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) UV–visible spectra of P-GNRs, (b) TEM image of as-synthesized P-GNRs.
(a) UV–visible spectra of N-GNRs, (b) TEM image of as-synthesized N-GNRs.
SEM-EDX and mapping results of P-GNRs.
SEM-EDX and mapping results of N-GNRs.
Effect of pH on peroxidase mimetic activity of GNRs (HAc buffer 0.1 mM, P and N-GNRs (10 μM), TMB (8 mM for P-GNRs and 1 mM for N-GNRs), and H2O2 (1 M for P-GNRs and 0.1 M for N-GNRs).
Effect of volume of GNRs (a) P-GNRs (50–300 μL) and (N-GNRs (25–200 μL) on peroxidase mimetic activity.
Optimization of reaction temperature for peroxidase mimetic activity of GNR (P-GNRs (10 μM), TMB (8 mM), and H2O2 (1 M)).
Optimization of H2O2 concentration for peroxidase mimetic activity of GNRs (a) P-GNRs, (b) N-GNRs.
Linear calibration curves of different concentrations of TMB versus peroxidase mimetic activity of GNRs at 450 nm (a) P-GNRs (TMB 6–8 mM), (b) N-GNRs (TMB 0.2–1 mM).

UV–visible spectra of peroxidase mimetic activity of GNRs incubated at 40 °C for 20 min in HAc buffer (0.1 M, pH 4) (a) TMB (8 mM), (b) TMB + H2O2 (8 mM + 1 M), (c) TMB + H2O2 + P-GNRs (8 mM + 1 M + 10 μM), (d) TMB + H2O2 + N-GNRs (0.1 mM + 0.1 M + 10 μM), (e) TMB + H2O2 + HRP (1 mM + 0.1 M +5 nM).
Digital image was taken before and after the peroxidase mimetic activity of GNRs (a) 0th min (b) 20 min, and (c) after addition of H2SO4.
Peroxidase mimetic activity of different NPs.

head.ce:title: Effect of surface charge on peroxidase mimetic activity of gold nanorods (GNRs)

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Centre for Nanobiotechnology, Vellore Institute of Technology, Vellore, India

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. Centre for Nanobiotechnology, Vellore Institute of Technology, Vellore, 632014, India.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In the present work, we have studied the impact of surface charge on the peroxidase mimetic activity of gold nanorods (GNRs). Gold nanorods with positive (cetyltrimethylammonium bromide (CTAB)) and negative (poly (styrene sulfonic acid) (PSS)) surface charges were synthesized and subsequently characterized by UV–visible spectroscopy, zeta potential analysis, and transmission electron microscopy (TEM). The two kinds of gold nanorods were evaluated for their peroxidase mimetic activity. Gold nanorods with different surface charges can able to mimic the enzyme activity near acidic pH over a wide range of temperatures. Poly (4-styrene sulfonic acid)-stabilized negative gold nanorods (N-GNRs) possessed higher peroxidase mimetic activity compared to CTAB-stabilized positive GNRs (P-GNRs) when tested with a low concentration of tetramethylbenzidine (1 mM) and hydrogen peroxide (0.1 M). The significant impact of surface charges of GNRs on the catalytic reactions may help us to make a prototype in creating nanozymes as peroxidase mimics.

ce:section.ce:para: Artificial enzymes are a major research topic at present because they overcome the drawbacks of natural enzymes [1]. For e.g. peroxidase, specifically, horseradish peroxidase (HRP) is able to activate hydrogen peroxide to carry out a variety of chemical reactions like oxidation. Natural enzymes have higher catalytic activity and higher specificity to substrate under mild reaction conditions [2]. Although the enzymes have efficient activity, their preparation and storage conditions are expensive. They can easily be digested by protease and denatured under high temperature and stringent conditions [3,4]. Therefore, it is highly necessary to prepare a simple, cost-effective, and highly stable enzyme mimetic system.

Yan's group first reported that iron oxide (Fe3O4) NPs exhibited a peroxidase mimetic activity, which opened the door for the development of NPs as enzyme mimetics in the bioanalysis field [5]. Since then, increasing attention has been given to the development of metal NPs as peroxidase mimetics. The peroxidase -like activity of NPs was studied using 3, 3ʹ, 5, 5ʹ-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) or 2, 2-azino-bis (3-ethylbenzothiazoline-6-sulfonic acid) (ABTS) as a substrate in the presence of hydrogen peroxide (H2O2). Typically, the nanomaterials acted as a peroxidase mimetics catalyze the decomposition of H2O2 that will oxidize the chromogenic substrate TMB.

The chemical equation (1) of the reaction between TMB and H2O2 is given below [6].
(1) NP + TMB + H2O2 → Ox-TMB + H2O …

Lie et al. study reported a strong temperature and pH-dependent peroxidase-like activity of porphyrin capped ceria NPs using TMB as a substrate [7]. Iridium NPs capped with polyvinylpyrrolidone reported to have both catalase and peroxidase mimetic activity [8]. Carbon coated Fe3O4 also reported to have a higher peroxidase mimetic activity as compared to bare Fe3O4 [9]. Yu et al. (2016) reported that polyvinylpyrrolidone functionalized stable molybdenum disulfide (MoS2) NPs exhibited similar peroxidase mimetic activity like HRP [10]. Zhao et al. (2015) reported a synthesis of stable ceria (CeO2) on the titanium oxide (CeO2@TiO2) nanocomposite and unpredictably it has an excellent peroxidase mimetic activity then ceria and TiO2 NPs [11]. Recently, many noble metal NPs like (gold (Au) [12,13], silver (Ag) [14], and platinum (Pt) [15]), metal oxides NPs such as cerium oxide (CeO2) [16], cobalt oxide (Co3O4) [17], iron oxide (Fe2O3, and Fe3O4) and metal alloys [18,19] were reported to show high peroxidase enzyme-like activity using TMB or ABTS as a substrate. The enzyme mimetic activity of NPs was confirmed by a visual color change and a maximal absorbance at a particular wavelength (ABTS (414 nm) [20], TMB (652 nm) [21]) using UV–visible spectroscopy.

Nanoparticles surface coated with functional compounds also takes part in an important function in enhanced peroxidase mimetic activity. Besides all the NPs, noble gold nanoparticles (GNPs) are highly reported to have peroxidase mimetic activity [12,22]. Compared to HRP, GNPs have many advantages like high stability against denaturation, tunable catalytic activity, and low-cost synthesis [4]. Recently, Khan et al. (2017) have reported that positively charged GNPs along with TMB–H2O2 showed a higher peroxidase-like activity as compared to citrate-stabilized GNPs [23]. At the same time, GNPs with different surface modifications i.e. with adenosine triphosphate (ATP) [24], mesoporous silica [25], and carbon nanotubes [26] were also reported to show enhanced catalytic activity. Metal composite [6] and bimetallic GNPs, for example, palladium-gold NPs [27,28] were also studied to analyze the peroxidase mimetic activity. However, only the spherical-shaped GNPs were highly explored as compared to other shapes. Liu et al. (2011) have reported that the structure of NPs is important to mimic the enzyme activity [29]. Since the enzyme mimetic reactions take place usually at the surface of the NPs, the active sites for the substrate might be occupied by absorbed materials that could lead to reduced catalytic activity. Compare to GNPs, GNRs were more stable due to their characteristic dual surface plasmon resonance (SPR) spectra (longitudinal and transverse), and it could be used for many applications like biosensor [30,31], and bio-imaging [32].

In catalytic reactions, the surface charge of the nanomaterials acts as an interface between the NPs and substrate, which could lead to enhanced enzyme mimetic activity [22,33,34]. El-Sayed et al. (2017) reported that the surface charge of materials is the most important factor responsible for the catalytic activity of GNPs [33]. This motivated us to study the effect of different charges on the peroxidase mimetic activity of GNRs. To test our hypothesis, we prepared positive and negatively charged GNRs (P and N-GNRs) using cetyltrimethyl ammonium bromide (CTAB) and poly (4-styrene sulfonic acid) (PSS) as surface capping agents. Then, we studied the peroxidase mimetic activity of different surface-charged GNRs (P and N) by using 3, 3’, 5, 5’-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) as a substrate in the presence of hydrogen peroxide (H2O2).

Cetyltrimethylammonium bromide (CTAB) (57-09-0), horseradish peroxidase (HRP) (900-99-0), poly (4-styrene sulfonic acid) (PSS) (28210-41-5), 3, 3’, 5, 5’-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) (54827-17-7) were procured from Sigma Aldrich. Hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) (7722-84-1) and sulphuric acid (H2SO4) (7664-93-9) were procured from SD Fine Chemicals Ltd., India, whereas, sodium acetate (127-09-3), and acetic acid (64-19-7) were purchased from Hi-Media Pvt. Ltd., India. Tetrachloroaurate hydrate (HAuCl4) (16961-25-4), silver nitrate (AgNO3) (7761-88-8), and sodium borohydride (NaBH4) (16940-66-2) were procured from SRL Pvt. Ltd., India. All the chemicals used here are analytical reagent grade.

P-GNRs were prepared with slight modification as reported by Chakraborty et al. (2018) [35]. In brief, the seed solution was prepared by the addition of ice-cold NaBH4 (0.3 mL, 0.01 M) to a mixture of HAuCl4 (2.5 mL, 0.5 mM) and CTAB (2.5 mL, 0.2 M) solutions under vigorous stirring condition. Brown-color seed solution was formed within 2 min, and the formed seed solution was incubated for 3 h. For preparing the growth solution, a mild reducing agent, ascorbic acid (1.4 mL, 0.079 M), was added to a mixture containing 200 mL of HAuCl4 (1 mM), 200 mL of CTAB (0.2 M), 1 mL of H2SO4 (0.5 M) and 6 mL of AgNO3 (0.004 M). After 2 min, 0.24 mL of seed solution was added to the above solution and incubated for 24 h. After 24 h of incubation, the color of the above mixture changed from colorless to dark brown color. The excess CTAB was removed by centrifugation at 9000 rpm for 30 min twice, and the resultant GNR suspension was stored at room temperature (28 °C) for further use.

N-GNRs were prepared as reported by Alex et al. (2017) [36]. P-GNRs were concentrated to 1/10th of the original volume by centrifugation. Change in the surface charge of GNRs was achieved by mild stirring with NaCl solution (8 mL, 1 mM) and PSS (133 μM) for 2 h at RT (28 °C). P-GNRs become modified to N-GNRs as PSS has a sulfonate group that gives a negative surface charge to GNRs. N-GNRs were centrifuged at 9000 rpm for 30 min and re-dispersed again in Milli-Q water for further use.

P and N-GNRs were characterized by a UV–visible spectrophotometer (Evolution 220, thermo scientific). The UV-visible spectra of GNRs were recorded in the wavelength range from 300 to 900 nm, and the recorded spectrum was re-plotted using GraphPad Prism. Milli-Q water was used as a blank. The morphology of the synthesized GNRs was observed under a high-resolution transmission electron microscope (HR-TEM; FEI Company Tecnai G2 20 Twin). The aspect ratio of GNRs was determined from the TEM images using Image J software. The surface elemental composition of the P- and N-GNRs were analyzed using SEM-EDX mapping (EVO-18 plus, Carl Zeiss, Germany). The surface charge of the GNRs was characterized using NanoBrook 90 Plus PALS Particle Size Analyzer (Brookhaven Instruments Corporation, USA). The concentration of GNRs was analyzed by inductively coupled plasma-optical emission spectroscopy (ICP-OES; Perkin Elmer Optima 5300 DV, USA).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Procedure for peroxidase mimetic activity measurement
ce:section.ce:para: The peroxidase mimetic activity of differently surface charged GNRs was examined using chromogenic TMB as a substrate. To investigate the peroxidase mimetic activity of P and N-GNRs, the reaction was carried out in the presence of H2O2 to observe the oxidation of typical peroxidase substrate TMB. The reaction mixture of 0.8 mL contains, TMB (8 mM for P and 1 mM for N-GNRs), H2O2 (1 M for P and 0.1 M for N-GNRs), 10 μM GNRs (P and N), and 0.1 M HAc (pH 4) buffer. The mixture was incubated for 20 min at 40 °C, after which H2SO4 (0.5 M) was added to the reaction mixture to stop the reaction. The catalytic activity for TMB and H2O2 can also be quantitatively analyzed using a UV-Visible spectrum. As a result, the reaction mixture will turn in to yellow, and then the absorbance at 450 nm will increase gradually based on the oxidation of TMB [37]. Therefore, the reaction mixture was analyzed using UV-visible absorption spectroscopy in the range of 300–900 nm. Depends on the level of optical density at 450 nm, the effect peroxidase activity of P and N-GNRs will be evaluated. The effect of primary experimental conditions like the concentration of GNRs, volume of GNRs, interaction time, temperature, pH, and the concentrations of TMB and H2O2 were also investigated for effective peroxidase mimetic activity of GNRs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of P and N-GNRs
ce:section.ce:para: P-GNRs were synthesized by a seed-mediated method using CTAB as the stabilizing agent. As–synthesized GNRs were primarily confirmed by a change in color from colorless to dark brown. N-GNRs were prepared by adding an optimum volume of PSS and NaCl to P-GNRs and the mixture was interacted for 1 h to prepare N-GNRs. The UV–visible spectra of both P and N-GNR suspension possess transverse (TSPR) and longitudinal surface plasmon resonance (LSPR) peaks at 520 and 696 nm, which confirmed the formation of P and N-GNRs (Figs. 1a and 2 a).

Both P and N-GNRs gave similar peaks with a slight change in the intensity. The concentration of as-synthesized GNRs analyzed by ICP-OES analysis was found to be 11.606 μM. Further, the morphology of the GNRs was observed using TEM. Figs. 1b and 2b showed a monodispersed rod-shaped P and N-GNRs, and the average aspect ratio was found to be 3.2.

Figs. 3 and 4
show the SEM – EDX along with mapping results of P-and N-GNRs, it validates the composition and presence of metallic elements present in the NRs (P and N-GNRs). As a result, extreme distribution of metallic gold (Au) was observed for P-GNRs (Fig. 3), for N-GNRs along with gold (Au), extreme distribution of sulfur (S) was observed which confirms the PSS functionalization of GNRs. Zeta potential analysis was done to confirm the surface charge of the GNRs (P and N). The zeta potential data of P-GNRs was observed to be +38 ± 1.24 mV due to the CTAB capping which contains the CTA+ group, responsible for the positive charge of GNRs [38]. The zeta potential result of N-GNRs was noted to be −37 ± 3.7 mV due to the sulfonate groups of PSS, which is responsible for the negative surface charge of GNRs [36,39,40]. As per the UV–visible spectra and TEM results, it was confirmed that the change in the surface charge from P to N-GNRs did not affect the size and shape of the as-synthesized GNRs.

Similar to that of horseradish peroxidase (HRP), the peroxidase mimetic activity of noble GNRs is dependent on several factors, like the pH of the reaction medium, the volume of GNRs, interaction temperature, and concentration of TMB and H2O2 [5,28,33,41]. The pH of the reaction matrix was considered an important factor for the peroxidase mimetic activity of GNRs. Sodium acetate (HAc) buffer was used as reaction matrix, and the influence of pH on the peroxidase mimetic activity of GNRs (10 μM) was investigated by varying the pH of HAc buffer (0.1 M, pH 1–8). The optimum reaction time selected for this study was 20 min (Fig. S1, Supplementary information). The reaction near-acidic condition (pH 4) was observed to show a high catalytic activity as compared to other neutral and basic pH (Fig. 5 ) [10,42]. Tian et al. (2015) reported that the maximized catalytic activity of porous ceria NPs was found in the pH range of 3.5–4.5, which was extremely close to that of HRP [37].

The reaction at basic pH was observed to show reduced catalytic activity, which might be due to the instability of H2O2 at alkaline pH [28]. Dong et al. (2015), reported that protons were one of the reactants, and an increase in the H+ concentration contributes to speeding up the catalytic reaction of NPs [43]. As per the results, peroxidase mimetic activity of GNRs observed to happen in near acidic condition. Similar results have been reported for various other nanoparticles as well for example positively charged gold nanoparticles [23], carbon dot- Pt nanocomposite [43], BSA stabilized Pt nanozymes [44], gold amalgam [45], and UiO-66(NH2) [46].

The reaction volume of the catalyst (GNRs) was tested in the range of 50–300 μL for P-GNRs and 25–200 μL for N-GNRs. The optimum concentration of GNRs utilized for this study was 10 μM (Fig. S2, Supplementary information). As a result, 200 μL of P-GNRs was observed to show higher catalytic activity than lower volumes (50, 100, and 150 μL) of P-GNRs, at the same, higher volumes (250 and 300 μL) showed similar activity as 200 μL. Hence, the volume of P-GNRs was selected as 200 μL for further reaction (Fig. 6 a).

For N-GNRs a lower volume of 100 μL showed higher activity as compared to P-GNRs, which might be due to the strong charge-based relationship between TMB and N-GNRs (Fig. 6b) [22]. As a result, a saturated peak was observed with the increase (<100 μL) in the volume of N-GNRs.

In the current work, the nanomaterials (P and N-GNRs) acts as a peroxidase mimetics, catalyze the decomposition of H2O2 that will oxidize the chromogenic substrate TMB. From Fig. 6a and b, 200 and 100 μL volume of P-GNRs and N-GNRs was sufficient enough to act as peroxidase mimetics to catalyze the decomposition of H2O2 that helped to oxidize the substrate TMB. An increase in the volume of P-GNRs (<200 μL) and N-GNRs (<100 μL) did not show any changes at 450 nm. Since the optimum volume of GNRs, P (200 μL) and N-GNRs (100 μL) was selected to accomplish the peroxidase activity under optimized conditions. The reaction temperature was optimized in the range from 20 to 40 °C. As per the results, an increase in the catalytic activity of GNRs was observed at 40 °C (Fig. 7 ). Similar result was observed for most of the previous studies for example peroxidase mimetic activity of citrate-capped silver NPs and UiO-66(NH2) NPs [14,46].
Thus, the optimized reaction conditions were pH 4, volumes of P-GNRs and N-GNRs – 200 and 100 μL, and interaction temperature at 40 °C.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of H2O2 and substrate concentration on the product with time
ce:section.ce:para: The catalytic oxidation of substrate TMB by NPs is H2O2 concentration-dependent [43]. In a catalytic study, H2O2 is an important intermediate, and the peroxidase mimetic activity of NPs originates from the decomposition of H2O2 [7,19]. Therefore, the optimization of H2O2 concentration was done for both P and N-GNRs. 1 M H2O2 was selected for P-GNRs when a series of concentrations of H2O2 (0.2–1 M) (Fig. 8 a) were tested.

Unexpectedly, for N-GNRs, H2O2 at 0.1 M concentration itself showed higher intensity at 450 nm when tested with H2O2 concentrations in the range 0.02–0.1 M (Fig. 8b). When the higher concentration of H2O2 (>0.1 M) was tested the catalytic activity of GNRs was observed to decrease at 450 nm (Fig. S3, Supplementary Information).

The linear calibration curve for the effect of H2O2 concentration (Fig. 8a and b) showed R2 values of 0.99 and 0.988 (P and N-GNRs), which confirmed that the peroxidase mimetic activity of P and N-GNRs were H2O2 concentration dependent. Jiang et al. (2014) study reported that apoferritin gold cluster NPs had a low affinity with H2O2 as a substrate as compared to HRP hence high concentration of H2O2 was utilized to obtain a maximal reaction velocity [41]. In the present study, N-GNRs (0.1 M) showed an excellent catalytic activity at a low concentration of H2O2 as compared to P-GNRs (1.M).

The effect of concentration of TMB and its oxidation in the presence of H2O2 (1 M for P-GNRs and 0.1 M for N-GNRs) was investigated. Optimization of chromogenic substrate TMB was tested in the range of 6–8 and 0.2–1 mM for P and N-GNRs, respectively. Observations were made that, the catalytic activity of P-GNRs increased gradually with increasing TMB concentrations, 8 mM TMB showed maximum activity and saturation of the reactivity (Fig. 9 a).

Higher concentration of TMB (8 mM) was required to mimic the peroxidase activity for P-GNRs due to less affinity towards positive charge (CTAB) on the surface of GNRs. At the same time, for N-GNRs, 1 mM concentration of TMB in presence of 0.1 M concentration of H2O2 was observed to show higher peroxidase mimetic activity (Fig. 9b). Haider et al. (2015) reported that parallel slopes revealing a ping pong mechanism and pointing that the proposed nanomaterials interact and respond well with the first substrate and then releases the first product prior to its reaction with the other substrate [26]. Hence, the absorbance of TMB is proportional to the concentration of H2O2. The aim of our experiment is to use a low concentration of H2O2, based on the optimum H2O2 concentrations (1 M (P-GNRs) and 0.1 M (N-GNRs)) 8 mM and 1 mM TMB were found to have an enhanced peroxidase activity. If we increase the concentration there was not much change in the absorbance, therefore, we have selected 8 mM (P-GNRs) and 1 mM TMB (N-GNRs) for our current study”.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Peroxidase mimetic activity of P and N-GNRs
ce:section.ce:para: The peroxidase mimetic activity of P and N-GNRs was evaluated spectroscopically by the catalytic oxidation of chromogenic substrate TMB in the presence of H2O2.

As shown in Fig. 10 , the chromogenic substrate TMB in the presence of H2O2 and (P and N)-GNRs formed high-intensity peaks as compared to TMB and TMB + H2O2. Yang et al. study also reported that a weak absorption peak was observed for TMB and H2O2 without NPs [41]. It confirmed that without GNRs, the system could not oxidize the TMB substrate effectively.
However, the results indicated that N-GNRs + TMB + H2O2 system has a higher catalytic activity as compared to the P-GNRs + TMB + H2O2 system.

This might be due to the charge characteristic of the chromogenic substrate TMB. He et al. (2011) reported that the PSS coating on gold–platinum NPs have high stability and maintain a high catalytic activity over wide pH and temperatures [47]. The functional sulphonate in the PSS coating might be one of the reasons for the high catalytic activity of N-GNRs as compared to P-GNRs. GNRs acted as an electron transfer mediator and initiated the reaction between H2O2 and TMB. TMB is a positively charged substrate holding two amine groups, which happened to have a higher affinity towards negatively charged materials/NPs than positively charged NPs [22]. Additionally, the peroxidase activity of natural enzyme HRP (1–7 nM) was also tested with an optimum TMB (1 mM) and H2O2 (0.1 M) concentration (Fig. S4, Supplementary information). As a result, HRP (5 nM) concentration was observed to have merely similar activity as P-GNRs, which confirmed the efficient peroxidase mimetic activity of N-GNRs.
The digital image of color changes before and after peroxidase mimetic activity of GNR is shown in Fig. 11 .

The charge based catalytic activity of GNRs was even confirmed by zeta potential analysis. Zeta potential analysis of GNRs (P and N) has been studied before and after the peroxidase mimetic reaction. The zeta potential analysis for substrate TMB was observed to be +12.67 ± 0.9 mV. The zeta potential results of P-GNRs before the catalytic reaction was +38 ± 1.24 mV, and after interacting with TMB it was observed to be +56 ± 0.97 mV. The increase in the net charge after the catalytic reaction in P-GNRs (+38 ± 1.24 to +56 ± 0.97 mV) was due to the presence of positively charged TMB. Both the P-GNRs and TMB with similar charge tend to repel a lot. This could be the reason P-GNRs showed less catalytic activity as compared to N-GNRs. However, the zeta potential value for N-GNRs before and after the peroxidase mimetic activity was observed to be −37.8 ± 3.7 and −15.18 ± 2.72 mV. In general, H+ has a high tendency to bind with NH2 on TMB to form NH3+ under acidic conditions [48]. The N-GNRs after the catalytic reactions showed a change in the net surface charge of N-GNRs from −37.8 to −15.18 ± 2.72 mV. These results proved that N-GNRs tend to have a strong electrostatic reaction with positively charged-TMB. In the present study, the negatively charged N-GNRs found to have more efficient peroxidase enzyme-like activity as compared to P-GNRs. This is mainly due to a strong electrostatic reaction between TMB and N-GNRs which was even confirmed by zeta potential analysis. However, Khan et at. (2016) reported the charge based comparative study to check the effect of peroxidase mimetic activity of GNPs. As result, the negatively charged citrate-capped GNPs observed to have a reduced peroxidase activity as compared to positively charged GNPs [23]. In the present work, along with the negative surface charge, the presence of sulphonate component in the PSS capping might be another reason for enhanced peroxidase-like activity of N-GNRs as compared to P-GNRs [22,33]. Chen et al. (2016) reported the poly (sodium 4-styrenesulfonate) (PSS) functionalized with reduced graphene oxide (rGO) has established an exceptional peroxidase activity as compared to bare rGO. The rGO NPs was highly stabilized by PSS due to bonding between aromatic rings of the polymer and the graphene surfaces in an edge to face interaction [48]. Table 1 shows the recently published research works on peroxidase-like activity of NPs. It depicted that N-GNR (GNR coated with PSS) observed to have high peroxidase mimetic activity as compared to other metal nanoparticles. It shows that N-GNRs readily catalyze the oxidation of 3, 3’, 5, 5’ – tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) in the presence of H2O2. In our experiment, the peroxidase mimetics activity of N-GNRs positively correlates with the absorbance at the oxidized TMB peak (λ 450 nm = 2.2, for TMB as reduction substrate). Here different metal nanoparticles like Pt, Mo, spherical Au, hollow porous Au, and Fe3O4 NPs observed to show very less absorbance, it directly correlated with peroxidase mimetic activity of the particular metal NPs. Compared to all the previous studies, our current study with PSS-functionalized N-GNRs observed to have enhanced peroxidase-like activity. Thus, it can be strongly emphasized that the negative surface charge of GNRs due to PSS functionalization plays a major role in the peroxidase mimetic activity of GNRs.

In the present study, we have studied the effect of surface charge on peroxidase mimetic activity of GNRs using TMB as a substrate. The peroxidase mimetic activity of GNRs (P and N) was influenced by optimizing the pH, interaction time, temperature, concentration of GNRs, TMB, and H2O2. As per the experimental results, N-GNRs observed show enhanced peroxidase mimetic activity as compared to P-GNRs. In this study, N-GNRs can able to mimic the peroxidase activity with less concentration of H2O2 (0.1 M) as compared to P-GNRs (1 M). Thus, the present work concludes that the enhanced peroxidase mimetic activity of GNRs depends on the surface charge of GNRs. The sulphonate in PSS provided the negative charge which acted as an intermediate between the substrate and GNRs. This model can be used to prepare a highly stable and cost-effective artificial enzyme, which can be used for biosensing and biomedical applications.
The authors declare that they have no conflicts of interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We acknowledge the management of Vellore Institute of Technology, Vellore for providing the Scanning and Transmission Electron Microscopy facility and other lab facilities to carry out this research work.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following is the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary Information_30.10.2018 Supplementary Information_30.10.2018

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.matchemphys.2019.02.015.
